summary: Security, data-integrity & concurrency deep audit of the FileID Windows engine+app
logs: Sec/integrity audit: 12 raw; 3 CONFIRMED (>=2/3 real); 2 contested

confirmed.title: Face-clustering phase-3 DELETE+re-INSERT silently discards People-tab edits committed during its lock-free window
confirmed.description:
handle_run_face_clustering is a three-phase pass: phase 1 (lines 47-172) snapshots the user's identities (names/title/is_unknown, the face->person map, and 'different people' verdicts) while holding the writer lock; phase 2 (lines 174-282) RELEASES the lock and spends multiple seconds doing pure in-memory cluster()+consolidate(); phase 3 (lines 288-367) RE-ACQUIRES the lock and runs `tx.execute("DELETE FROM persons", [])` (line 293) + `UPDATE face_prints SET person_id = NULL` (line 292), then re-creates persons rows from the PHASE-1 snapshot.

The engine-side single-flight gate (`face_cluster_active`, main.rs:787) only stops a SECOND clustering run. It does NOT gate the People-tab bulk handlers, which acquire the same writer mutex independently on their own tokio tasks (main.rs:629-675, no `face_cluster_active` check): handle_rename_person (bulk.rs:549 `UPDATE persons SET ...`), handle_merge_clusters (bulk.rs:450 `DELETE FROM persons WHERE id`), handle_mark_persons_as_unknown (bulk.rs:587 `UPDATE persons SET is_unknown=1, name=NULL`), and trash.rs revertMerge.

Because phase 2 holds no lock, a bulk edit can acquire the writer, commit a rename/merge/mark-unknown into `persons`/`face_prints.person_id`, and then phase 3's unconditional `DELETE FROM persons` + re-INSERT (built from the pre-phase-2 snapshot, with no optimistic-concurrency/generation check) silently throws that edit away. The user sees their just-applied name/merge vanish with no error.
confirmed.trigger: Auto-clustering fires on EVERY ScanComplete (and on Failed) via EngineClient.Apply -> AutoTriggerFaceClusteringAsync (EngineClient.cs:1104, 1066); incremental rescans are supported and re-emit ScanComplete. Concrete sequence: (1) user is in the People tab and renames a person (or merges two clusters); (2) a background/incremental rescan completes and auto-fires RunFaceClustering; (3) clustering enters phase 2 (lock-free, seconds-long on a large face set); (4) the user's renamePerson/mergeClusters bulk handler grabs the freed writer lock and commits; (5) phase 3 re-acquires the lock, DELETEs persons, and re-INSERTs from the stale phase-1 snapshot -> the rename/merge is permanently lost. PeopleViewModel has no in-flight-clustering guard, so nothing blocks the edit on either side.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Treat the clustering persist transaction and concurrent person edits as mutually exclusive at the application layer (the DB mutex alone cannot, because clustering intentionally drops it across phase 2). Two viable approaches: (a) gate the person-mutating bulk handlers (renamePerson, mergeClusters, markPersonsAsUnknown, markPersonsDifferent, revertMerge) on the same `face_cluster_active` flag so they bounce/await while a clustering pass is in flight, mirroring the wipe interlock in wipe.rs; or (b) make phase 3 re-validate before clobbering: re-read the identity snapshot inside the phase-3 transaction (after re-acquiring the lock) instead of reusing the phase-1 capture, and/or bump a `persons` generation counter in phase 1 and abort+retry the persist if any person row changed during phase 2. Option (b) is the minimal-correctness fix and also closes the analogous lost-update for any future lock-free phase.

verdicts.reasoning:
Independently re-derived and confirmed. The lock IS dropped across the multi-second clustering work: face_clustering.rs:171 `drop(conn)` ends phase 1; it is not re-acquired until line 288. cluster() (202) and consolidate() (269) run lock-free, as the comments explicitly state. Phase 3 then unconditionally runs `UPDATE face_prints SET person_id = NULL` (292) + `DELETE FROM persons` (293) and re-INSERTs identity (338-355) reconstructed ENTIRELY from the phase-1 snapshot (prior_by_person + face_to_prior captured at lines 129-169) — no re-read, no generation/optimistic-concurrency check.

The person-mutating bulk handlers each spawn an independent tokio task (main.rs:629-675) and acquire the same parking_lot writer mutex (handle_rename_person bulk.rs:533/549; handle_mark_persons_as_unknown bulk.rs:580/587; handle_merge_clusters bulk.rs:444/450; handle_revert_merge trash.rs:196). NONE check face_cluster_active — that flag gates only a second RunFaceClustering (main.rs:787) and the wipe interlock (wipe.rs:48). Concurrency is genuine: handle_line is awaited serially but every handler returns immediately after tokio::spawn, and both clustering and bulk work run in spawn_blocking worker threads contending on one mutex; a bulk commit landing during phase 2 is invisible to phase 3's stale snapshot.

Reachability is confirmed: AutoTriggerFaceClusteringAsync fires unconditionally on every ScanComplete (EngineClient.cs:1104) and on Failed (1066); incremental rescans re-emit ScanComplete. PeopleViewModel has no in-flight-clustering guard (verified), and the person-edit Async methods are bare SendCommandAsync calls (EngineClient.Commands.cs:644-754) with no interlock on either side.

Harm is real and permanent for renamePerson and markPersonsAsUnknown: phase 3 re-attaches the OLD name / is_unknown=0 from the phase-1 snapshot, silently discarding the edit the user just committed — genuine, silent user-data loss with no error surfaced. mergeClusters/revertMerge are likewise clobbered structurally. One correction to the report: markPersonsDifferent writes to face_verifications, which phase 3 does NOT delete, so that verdict row survives and is only missed by THIS run's consolidation (the blocked set was read in phase 1) — transient, not permanent loss; the report overstates that single handler.

Verdict 'real': genuinely triggerable under normal use (no attacker needed — the auto-trigger is the trigger) AND genuinely harmful (silent data loss of user identity edits). Confidence held at 0.8 rather than higher only because the exploit window is timing-narrow (the user must commit an edit during the seconds-long phase-2 of a freshly auto-fired pass), so it is reachable but not constant; the loss when it does occur is real and unrecoverable. Suggested fix (a) gating the person-mutating handlers on face_cluster_active, or (b) re-reading the snapshot inside the phase-3 transaction, is sound.

Verified the full call chain; the claim is accurate and reachable, and the consequence is permanent silent loss of user-entered identity data (a wrong-state-persisted / data-loss outcome under this lens).

THE RACE (confirmed against source):
- face_clustering.rs is exactly the documented three-phase pass. Phase 1 (lines 47-172) snapshots identities into `prior_by_person` (line 153) and `face_to_prior` (line 166) while holding db.lock(), then drops the lock (line 171). Phase 2 (lines 174-282) runs cluster()+consolidate() with NO lock and NO DB access. Phase 3 re-acquires db.lock() (line 288), opens an unchecked_transaction, and unconditionally runs `UPDATE face_prints SET person_id = NULL` (line 292) + `DELETE FROM persons` (line 293), then re-INSERTs persons (lines 338-355) using ONLY the phase-1 snapshot. There is NO re-read of persons in phase 3, NO generation/optimistic-concurrency check, NO abort/retry. tx.commit() (367) succeeds on stale data.
- The engine single-flight gate (main.rs:787-799) only bounces a SECOND RunFaceClustering. The person-mutating bulk handlers are spawned independently with NO face_cluster_active check: RenamePerson (main.rs:629-639 → bulk.rs:549 `UPDATE persons`), MergeClusters (612-622 → bulk.rs:450 `DELETE FROM persons WHERE id` + bulk.rs:447 person_id move), MarkPersonsAsUnknown (640-650 → bulk.rs:587 `UPDATE persons SET is_unknown=1, name=NULL`), MarkPersonsDifferent (665-675), and revertMerge (trash.rs:205-222, re-INSERT + person_id updates). Each grabs the same writer mutex on its own tokio task during phase 2's lock-free window.

CONCRETE LOST UPDATE: person #5="Alice" snapshotted in phase 1 → user renames to "Alice Smith" during phase 2 (commits) → phase 3 DELETEs persons (drops "Alice Smith") and re-INSERTs inheriting name from stale prior_by_person[5]="Alice". The rename is permanently gone; the next clustering run snapshots the already-clobbered table, so there is no recovery. Not rolled back, not logged-and-skipped, not cosmetic.

TRIGGERABILITY: AutoTriggerFaceClusteringAsync fires on EVERY ScanComplete (EngineClient.cs:1104) and on Failed (1066); incremental rescans re-emit ScanComplete. App-side `_faceClusterAutoInFlight` (1273) only dedups auto-triggers — it does NOT gate person edits. PeopleViewModel has zero clustering-in-flight guard (grep found none), and edits send straight via SendCommandAsync (EngineClient.Commands.cs:644-654). So nothing blocks the edit on either side. Phase 2 is multi-second on large face sets (the entire stated reason for dropping the lock; 3-pass kNN density clustering + consolidate over up to tens of thousands of faces), so the window is realistically hittable.

SMOKING GUN: wipe.rs (lines 37-51) ALREADY waits on face_cluster_active before truncating, with a comment describing this EXACT lock-free-window hazard ("clustering persist would re-INSERT phantom persons rows built from its pre-wipe in-memory anchors"). The maintainers recognized and guarded the identical race for wipe but did not extend the interlock to the person-edit handlers — strong corroboration that the window is real and the gap is unintentional. The suggested fix (gate the bulk handlers on face_cluster_active, mirroring wipe; or re-read/generation-check in phase 3) is sound and would not break legitimate cases.

Confidence held at 0.82 (not higher) because it is a timing race, not a deterministic bug: the edit must commit inside the multi-second phase-2 window, so it won't fire on every interaction. But it requires no special privilege, follows a plausible real workflow (rescan completes while user is renaming/merging in People), and silently destroys user-entered names/merges — squarely a high-severity integrity defect.

Independently verified the full call chain; the race and its harm are genuine, and the suggested fixes are safe (the fix-safety lens does not refute it).

STRUCTURE (face_clustering.rs): handle_run_face_clustering is exactly the three-phase pass described. Phase 1 reads under the writer lock and DROPS it at line 171. Phase 2 (174-282) holds NO lock and runs cluster()+consolidate() purely in-memory (multi-second on a large face set). Phase 3 re-acquires the lock at line 288 and unconditionally runs `UPDATE face_prints SET person_id=NULL` (292) + `DELETE FROM persons` (293), then re-INSERTs persons rows built ONLY from the phase-1 snapshots prior_by_person/face_to_prior (300-358). Phase 3 never re-reads the live persons table.

GATE GAP: face_cluster_active is consulted in only two places — the RunFaceClustering single-flight (main.rs:787) and the wipe interlock (wipe.rs:48). The person-mutating bulk handlers acquire the same writer mutex with NO such check: handle_merge_clusters (bulk.rs:444 lock, 447 UPDATE face_prints SET person_id, 450 DELETE FROM persons), handle_rename_person (bulk.rs:533/549), handle_mark_persons_as_unknown (bulk.rs:580/587), handle_mark_persons_different (bulk.rs:648). Each runs on its own tokio task (main.rs:619/636/647/672).

TRIGGER: auto-clustering fires on every ScanComplete (EngineClient.cs:1104) and on Failed (1066); rescan is supported and re-emits ScanComplete (EngineClient.cs:110). No app-side in-flight guard blocks People-tab edits (PeopleViewModel has none; RunFaceClusteringAsync is fire-and-forget, EngineClient.Commands.cs:299). A bulk handler's db.lock() during phase 2 (lock held by nobody) succeeds immediately, commits, and phase 3 then re-acquires and clobbers from the stale snapshot. Fully reachable.

HARM (permanent): A rename committed in phase 2 is overwritten by the phase-1 name in phase 3 and is NOT recovered next run (the live row now holds the old name). A merge is worse — phase 3 re-splits the user-joined clusters by geometry, and the merge intent is persisted nowhere (only same_person=0 'different' verdicts survive in face_verifications, not 'same person' merges), so it is permanently lost. The wipe.rs comment (38-46) already documents this exact lock-free-window class for the analogous wipe race, confirming the project recognizes the hazard.

FIX-SAFETY (assigned lens): Both proposals are correct and break no legitimate workflow. Fix (a) modeled on the existing wipe interlock (bounded await then proceed) delays an edit by up to ~5s but still applies it — no valid edit is falsely rejected if implemented as await rather than a hard busy-bounce. Fix (b) (re-read the identity snapshot inside the phase-3 transaction, or a persons generation-counter abort/retry) is the minimal-correctness fix and preserves the legitimate carry-forward-names intent the phase-1 snapshot exists for — reading live persons at phase-3 time is strictly fresher and still carries names forward. Neither fix rejects a valid case, so the fix-safety lens does not refute the framing.

Confidence is high but not maximal because the window requires the user to be actively editing in the People tab at the moment an (often incremental) rescan completes — a narrow-ish timing window in practice — but it is a real, unguarded lost-update with no compensating mechanism, so it qualifies as real.

confirmed.title: BGE text-embed pooling trusts model-reported sequence/batch dims → index-OOB panic kills a scan worker on a malformed ONNX
confirmed.description: The mean-pool loop derives `seq = shape[1]` directly from the model's output tensor (line 136) and then indexes `enc.attention_mask[t]` for `t in 0..seq` (line 140). `enc.attention_mask` has length `n = enc.ids.len()` — the number of tokens the tokenizer produced and fed to the model (the input is built with shape `(1, n)`, lines 89-97). A well-formed BGE export returns `last_hidden_state` of shape `(1, n, 384)` so `seq == n`, but the only validation performed is `shape.len() != 3 || shape[2] != HIDDEN` (line 130). Neither `shape[0]` (batch) nor `shape[1]` (seq vs n) is checked. A model whose graph emits a sequence dimension larger than the input token count makes `enc.attention_mask[t]` index past the end of the Vec → panic. Separately, an output with `shape[0] == 0` leaves `data` empty while `seq > 0`, so `data[t * HIDDEN + h]` (line 146) also panics. The build profile is `panic = "unwind"` (Cargo.toml [profile.release] explicitly forbids abort), and `embed()` is called inline from `process_file_predecoded` (tagging.rs:1251-1253), which itself runs directly inside the worker task's `while let Ok(predecoded) = rx.recv().await` loop (tagging.rs:782-799) — NOT inside a per-file `tokio::spawn`. A panic here unwinds the whole worker task, so that scan worker dies for the remainder of the scan (and any other worker that touches the same shared `Mutex<BgeText>` and the same malformed output dies the same way), silently degrading or stalling document indexing.
confirmed.trigger: User downloads/installs a crafted or buggy BGE-small ONNX re-export into %LOCALAPPDATA%/FileID/Models/bge_text/ (the threat model explicitly includes a malicious model the user happened to download). The model loads and warms up fine on the canned "warmup" string (which happens to round-trip seq==n), then on a real document whose token count differs it returns last_hidden_state with shape[1] > n (e.g. a padded/concat output) or shape[0]==0. The first Doc/PDF file scanned triggers the OOB panic and unwinds the scan worker.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Validate the full output shape before pooling and clamp the loop to the safe minimum. After line 130, also require `shape[0] >= 1` and bound the loop: `let seq = (shape[1] as usize).min(enc.attention_mask.len());` (and verify `data.len() >= seq * HIDDEN`), bailing with a clear error otherwise. This turns a hostile/buggy model into a per-file `Err` the caller already handles (tagging.rs:1255-1263 logs and continues) instead of a worker-killing panic.

verdicts.reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL (worker-killing panic), with one factual correction to the claim's narrative that does not change the conclusion.

Two distinct, genuinely-unguarded panic vectors in BgeText::embed (bge_text.rs):
1) attention_mask OOB (line 140): seq = shape[1] is taken straight from the model output (line 136). enc.attention_mask has length n = tokenizer token count (lines 89,95). The ONLY validation (line 130) is shape.len()==3 && shape[2]==HIDDEN — it never checks shape[1]==n or shape[0]. A model emitting shape[1] > n makes enc.attention_mask[t] index past the Vec end → index-OOB panic.
2) data OOB (line 146): I confirmed in the actual ort 2.0.0-rc.10 source that try_extract_tensor returns a slice of length shape.num_elements() (extract.rs:158), and num_elements() multiplies all dims, returning 0 if any dim is ≤0/negative (tensor/mod.rs:34-43). So shape[0]==0 (or a dynamic/-1 dim) yields empty data while seq=shape[1]>0 → data[t*HIDDEN+h] panics.

Reachability confirmed: BGE is loaded into models.bge_text (Some) when weights+vocab exist at the fixed local path %LOCALAPPDATA%/FileID/Models/bge_text/ (tagging.rs:499-507). embed() is called inline in process_file_predecoded for Doc/Pdf with non-empty extracted text (tagging.rs:1253). The surrounding match handles only Err (1253-1264) — a panic bypasses it. The catch_unwind at tagging.rs:1106 wraps ONLY image decode, not BGE. panic="unwind" is intentional (Cargo.toml:239-245). Model integrity checking is optional (expected_sha256: Option<String>, downloader.rs:127), and the verifier instructions explicitly place a user-downloaded malicious/buggy model in scope.

CORRECTION to the claim: it states the embed is "NOT inside a per-file tokio::spawn." That is factually wrong — it runs inside tokio::spawn (tagging.rs:772) wrapped in tokio::time::timeout (line 794). However this does not defeat the claim: tokio::time::timeout does not catch panics (only the Elapsed branch, line 801); the panic unwinds the spawned worker task, terminating its `while let Ok(predecoded) = rx.recv().await` loop (line 782). That worker is dead for the rest of the scan; the harmful degradation/stall of document indexing is real. Other workers share the same Mutex<BgeText> and hit the identical malformed output, dying in turn.

The suggested fix converts a worker-killing panic into a per-file Err already handled at tagging.rs:1255-1263 and does not break well-formed exports (shape == (1, n, 384)). Confidence 0.85: code logic is airtight; the small deduction is for the trigger requiring a non-default user action (installing a non-checksummed crafted/buggy ONNX) rather than ordinary untrusted file content.

The mechanical bug is REAL and I reproduced the logic, but it does not clear the data-loss/integrity/engine-panic bar under the real threat model.

What I verified in code:
- bge_text.rs:89 `n = enc.ids.len()`; wordpiece_tokenizer.rs:84-86 sets `attention_mask = vec![1; ids.len()]`, so `enc.attention_mask.len() == n` exactly. The input tensors are built `(1, n)` (bge_text.rs:90-97).
- The only output validation (bge_text.rs:130) is `shape.len()!=3 || shape[2]!=HIDDEN`. It does NOT pin `shape[1]` to `n` nor require `shape[0]>=1`.
- Pool loop: `seq = shape[1]` (136), `enc.attention_mask[t]` for `t in 0..seq` (140) → OOB panic if `seq > n`; `data[t*HIDDEN+h]` (146) → OOB if `shape[0]==0` (empty data, seq>0). Both are genuine `Vec` index panics.
- Call chain confirms no isolation: the worker is a single fire-and-forget `tokio::spawn` (tagging.rs:772) running a `while let` loop (782-852); `process_file_predecoded` is awaited directly via `tokio::time::timeout` (793-799) with NO per-file spawn and NO catch_unwind on this path (the only catch_unwind, 1106, is the image-decode path). `embed()` is called inline at 1252-1253. So a panic unwinds the entire worker task.
- Cargo.toml:239 confirms `panic="unwind"`, so the process survives; the worker task dies as a JoinError. Consequence = one (or, per shared Mutex<BgeText>, several) scan workers permanently gone for the rest of the scan → degraded/stalled doc embedding.
- The codebase elsewhere DOES guard this exact shape: clip_text.rs:135 checks divisibility and 141 derives `dim` from actual `data.len()`; clip_text `embed` (90) just `data.to_vec()`s. BGE's pooling is the outlier — so the suggested clamp (`seq.min(attention_mask.len())` + `data.len() >= seq*HIDDEN` + `shape[0]>=1`) is correct and harmless.

Why it is REFUTED under the data-loss/integrity lens:
1. No data loss/corruption/wrong-state persisted: the panic fires BEFORE any DB emit for that file (`tx.send(tagged)` at 845 is never reached). The file is simply left unindexed; no row is corrupted or mis-written; it re-processes on next scan. No telemetry/network involvement.
2. It does NOT panic the engine — `panic="unwind"` is deliberate (Cargo.toml:239-245) precisely so a task panic is isolated and the engine keeps serving. The dropped `out_tx` clone doesn't deadlock the out channel (other workers still feed it). The harm is a recoverable availability/throughput dip, not a crash or corruption.
3. Trigger is gated behind a non-default sideload: the supported BGE install is SHA256-pinned (registry.rs:454 `828e1496…`) and downloader-verified (downloader.rs:346,593), and that canonical Xenova export round-trips `seq==n`. To hit the bug the user must manually drop a crafted/buggy `.onnx` (or a misbehaving `_int8`/`_qnn` variant) into %LOCALAPPDATA%/FileID/Models/bge_text/, which load() (39-82) loads with only existence+warmup checks.

So: a genuine hardening gap worth fixing (unguarded OOB the team guards elsewhere), but under the strict lens it neither corrupts/loses stored data, persists wrong state, leaks over the network, nor panics the engine. Severity is over-claimed ("high", "engine-panic kills a scan worker silently degrading indexing") — the real impact is a recoverable, restart-healed degradation behind a sideloaded model, i.e. medium/low robustness, not a data-integrity/engine-panic incident. Confidence 0.7 (the defect and call-chain are certain; the downgrade hinges on the lens bar and on the official model being hash-locked).

Verified the full call chain and the ort 2.0.0-rc.10 tensor contract; the claim holds and the suggested fix is safe.

REACHABILITY (confirmed): bge_text.rs:130 only validates `shape.len()==3 && shape[2]==384`. Neither shape[0] (batch) nor shape[1] (seq) is checked against the input. The output shape is entirely model-controlled and need not equal the input token count.
- attention_mask OOB (line 140): `enc.attention_mask` has length n==ids.len() (wordpiece_tokenizer.rs:84, `vec![1; ids.len()]`, n>=2). `seq=shape[1] as usize` (line 136). A model emitting (1, seq, 384) with seq>n makes `enc.attention_mask[t]` panic at t==n.
- data OOB (line 146): I verified extract.rs:158 builds the data slice as `slice::from_raw_parts(ptr, shape.num_elements())`, and tensor/mod.rs:34-43 returns 0 from num_elements() if any dim is negative (and is the dim product otherwise). So shape[0]==0 ⇒ data.len()==0 while seq>0 ⇒ `data[t*HIDDEN+h]` panics on an empty slice. A negative shape[1] also yields empty data + huge usize seq ⇒ panic.

HARM (confirmed): embed() is called inline (tagging.rs:1253) from process_file_predecoded, which is awaited directly inside a fire-and-forget `tokio::spawn` worker (tagging.rs:772-799) — NOT a per-file nested spawn. The only catch_unwind (tagging.rs:1106) wraps image decode only, not this path. Cargo.toml:239 explicitly sets `panic = "unwind"` (not abort). A panic unwinds the worker task; its JoinHandle is discarded, so the worker dies permanently and stops draining the predecoded channel — silently degrading/stalling doc indexing. All workers share one Arc<Mutex<BgeText>> (models.bge_text), so each worker hitting the malformed output dies identically.

THREAT MODEL (valid): weights load from user-writable %LOCALAPPDATA%/FileID/Models/bge_text/ (paths.rs:50) with no shape integrity check (only weights.exists(), bge_text.rs:41). The documented threat model includes a downloaded/swapped model.

FIX-SAFETY (the requested lens — fix is correct, no false positives): For a well-formed BGE export returning (1, n, 384) with n==attention_mask.len(): shape[0]>=1 passes; seq=min(shape[1], attention_mask.len())==n is a no-op; data.len()==n*384 >= seq*384 passes. The clamp/checks only ever fire on pathological output. n is bounded by MAX_SEQ=256 (tokenizer truncates), and token_type_id-less exports are unaffected (the fix touches only the pooling loop). The fix converts the panic into the per-file Err the caller already logs-and-continues on (tagging.rs:1255-1263). No legitimate case is broken.

Corroborating evidence the omission is a real inconsistency, not an invariant: the sibling clip_text.rs:135 deliberately does NOT trust the reported shape — it validates `data.len() % batch` and derives dim from data.len(). bge_text.rs is the outlier that trusts shape[1].

Confidence is high but not 1.0 because the most-cited 'warmup passes then a real doc panics' sub-scenario requires a model whose output seq dim varies with input in a specific way; a constant-bad-shape model would instead panic at warmup (still a panic, but at load-time, unwinding the scan-setup task rather than the per-file Err the load code's match intends). Either way the underlying defect — unvalidated, model-controlled dims feeding two unchecked index sites under panic=unwind on a non-spawn-isolated worker — is genuinely triggerable and harmful.

confirmed.title: restoreFromTrash restores an arbitrary item when multiple Recycle Bin entries share the same original path
confirmed.description: The PowerShell restore matches Recycle Bin items solely on (`System.Recycle.DeletedFrom` == parent AND `Name` == filename) and `break`s on the FIRST match. Windows permits many distinct deleted files to share the same original path — every time the user trashes a file at C:\Pics\cat.jpg and later re-creates+re-trashes another cat.jpg, the bin accumulates multiple `$R*` entries all reporting DeletedFrom=C:\Pics and Name=cat.jpg. trash_log records only `original_path` (recycle_bin_id is always None at write time in bulk.rs handle_trash_files), so the restore has no way to disambiguate and brings back an arbitrary (typically newest-or-oldest, shell-enumeration-order-dependent) item — potentially the wrong file's bytes — then reports success and re-inserts the DB row as if the intended file came back. The user believes they recovered batch X but may have recovered a same-named item from an unrelated earlier trash batch.
confirmed.trigger: Trash C:\folder\a.jpg; recreate and trash a different a.jpg at the same path; restore the FIRST batch. The shell enumeration picks whichever same-(path,name) item it hits first, not the one in the requested batch.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Persist a durable disambiguator at trash time and match on it during restore: e.g. populate TrashLogItem.recycle_bin_id with the bin's `$I`/`$R` identity (or the item's deletion timestamp via System.Recycle.DateDeleted) and require both DeletedFrom+Name AND that identity/timestamp to match before InvokeVerb. Without a unique key, restore-by-path cannot honor the per-batch contract the trash log promises.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified every load-bearing claim against the code:

1. No disambiguator is ever captured at trash time. bulk.rs:377 hardcodes `recycle_bin_id: None` in the only TrashLogItem write path (handle_trash_files). The shell layer cannot supply one: shell/trash.rs `trash_path` returns `Result<()>` and `trash()` returns `Vec<bool>` — the assigned $R* name / DateDeleted is never propagated back. trash_log.rs:24-28 even documents recycle_bin_id as "Often empty; restore by path is the canonical fallback." So the log records only original_path + file_id + timestamp.

2. Restore matches solely on (DeletedFrom==parent AND Name==filename) and breaks on the first hit. trash.rs:153-159: `foreach ($i in $bin.Items()) { if ($loc -eq $wantParent -and $nm -eq $wantName) { $i.InvokeVerb('Undelete'); break } }`. `$i.Name` is the un-mangled original display name, so two distinct deleted files from the same folder with the same name both satisfy the predicate. Windows genuinely permits multiple bin entries sharing one original path (each delete creates a distinct $R<rand> entry), so the matcher cannot honor the per-batch contract that restoreFromTrash is keyed on (payload.batch_id → trash_log::read_batch).

3. The post-restore guard does not mitigate it. trash.rs:179 only checks that *some* file now exists at original_path; it cannot distinguish the intended bytes from a same-named sibling, so it reinforces the false "success" report (succeeded += 1 at trash.rs:100, plus an INSERT OR IGNORE DB row at trash.rs:87-99 and message=original_path).

Threat-model/skeptical lens: This is a logic/disambiguation defect, not an injection — no attacker input is needed and SEC-7 path containment + HMAC are intact and irrelevant to the bug. It is triggerable by ordinary user action: trash file A at P, recreate+retrash a different file at P, then restore the older batch by id. The UI exposes per-batch undo by batch_id and the log retains up to 1024 batches (MAX_ENTRIES), so deliberately restoring a non-latest batch is realistic. Shell enumeration order is non-deterministic, so the recovered item is arbitrary.

Harm classification: NOT data-loss/corruption (restore is additive; InvokeVerb('Undelete') restores to the original path and Windows auto-renames on collision — no bytes overwritten/destroyed; both files survive) and NOT telemetry/panic. It IS a genuine data-integrity wrong-result: the user recovers an unrelated batch's file content while the engine reports the intended file as restored and writes a DB row for it. That matches the claim's stated "medium / data-integrity" severity precisely. The suggested fix (persist DateDeleted or the $I/$R identity at trash time and require it to match before InvokeVerb) is sound and would not regress the common single-instance case.

Confidence held at 0.72 (not higher) because the impact is bounded wrong-recovery rather than destructive loss, and the trigger requires the user to restore a non-latest same-path batch — uncommon, though fully supported by the API and a real violation of the documented per-batch undo contract. Verified files: platforms/windows/src/engine/src/commands/trash.rs (lines 11-129 handler, 131-183 restore_one_from_recycle_bin), commands/trash_log.rs (20-29 TrashLogItem, 88-118 read_batch), commands/bulk.rs (305-417 handle_trash_files, esp. 374-378), shell/trash.rs (30-139).

VERIFIED against the real code and call chain.

Mechanics confirmed:
- trash.rs:131-183 `restore_one_from_recycle_bin` matches Recycle Bin items only on `System.Recycle.DeletedFrom == $wantParent` AND `$i.Name == $wantName` (lines 154-156) and `break`s on the FIRST match (line 157). No deletion-timestamp or $R/$I identity is used.
- The only durable disambiguator field, `TrashLogItem.recycle_bin_id` (trash_log.rs:21-29), is structurally always None: it is hardcoded `recycle_bin_id: None` at write time in bulk.rs:377 (handle_trash_files), and the shell trash layer (shell/trash.rs trash_path) fires `IFileOperation::DeleteItem` without ever calling GetName/capturing the bin identity. The field is effectively dead.
- Restore is keyed solely on batch_id (ipc/mod.rs:378-384 RestoreFromTrashPayload), which read_batch (trash_log.rs:88-118) expands to a list of original_path strings — so restore can only match by path, never by which physical bin entry belongs to the requested batch.
- The post-restore guard at trash.rs:179 only checks that *a* file exists at original_path (Path::exists), not *which* file — it cannot detect wrong bytes. On the first match it InvokeVerb('Undelete'), then the engine re-INSERTs a DB row (trash.rs:87-99) and reports succeeded (line 100), regardless of which item's bytes actually landed.

Windows behavioral premise is accurate: the Shell.Application Recycle Bin namespace (0x0a) reports `$i.Name` as the original display name and `DeletedFrom` as the original parent, and Windows retains multiple distinct deleted files that shared the same original full path as separate entries, all reporting identical (DeletedFrom, Name). So a (path,name) collision in the bin genuinely exists when a same-named file is re-created and re-trashed before the bin is emptied.

Data-integrity consequence: this is persistent wrong-state, not cosmetic/recovered/rolled-back. The wrong file's bytes are restored to the original path, success is reported, and a DB row is created as if the intended file returned — silently violating the per-batch restore contract. No telemetry/network leak and no engine panic, so this is data-integrity, not the other harm classes.

Severity sits at medium: the trigger requires a specific but plausible sequence (trash a path, re-create+re-trash a DIFFERENT file at the same full path, both coexisting in the bin, then restore the older batch). For FileID's workload — bulk-organizing tens of thousands of files, Cleanup-tab bulk-trashing same-named duplicates, re-imported same-named media like IMG_0001.jpg/invoice.pdf — same-(path,name) bin collisions are a recurring real-world condition, and the bin is not auto-emptied between ops. The suggested fix (persist DateDeleted / $R-$I identity at trash time and require it to match before InvokeVerb) is sound and would not break legitimate single-entry restores. Confidence is high on the code defect; slightly below maximal only because exploitation depends on the user's deletion history rather than being a default-path certainty.

The described behavior is technically accurate at the code level, but the claimed severity is overstated and the suggested fix is partly unsafe — so it does not clearly meet the "real" bar (genuinely triggerable AND genuinely harmful under the data-loss/corruption threat model).

VERIFIED FACTS:
- bulk.rs:377 hardcodes recycle_bin_id: None; shell/trash.rs uses IFileOperation::DeleteItem (line 62) and captures no $R*/$I identity or DateDeleted, so no disambiguator exists at write time. The trash_log.rs:24-28 doc comment itself states recycle_bin_id is "Often empty; restore by path is the canonical fallback" — the path-based contract is intentional.
- Restore (trash.rs:153-159) matches only on System.Recycle.DeletedFrom == parent AND $i.Name == filename, then break on first enumeration match. $i.Name is the original display name, so multiple bin entries sharing the same original path all match; only the first-enumerated one is restored.
- Windows genuinely allows multiple distinct bin entries with identical DeletedFrom+Name (each gets a unique $I/$R pair), so the ambiguity is real and reproducible via the stated trigger (trash a.jpg, recreate+retrash a different a.jpg, restore the earlier batch).

WHY NOT "real":
1. Bounded harm — NOT data-loss/corruption. The non-restored same-named item stays in the bin (still recoverable from Explorer); the restored file lands at the correct path with the correct name. The only defect is that the restored bytes may be the wrong VERSION. No file is destroyed, overwritten, or corrupted; no panic; no telemetry. The post-restore existence guard (trash.rs:179-181) doesn't catch wrong-version but there is no loss to catch.

FIX-SAFETY (my assigned lens) — the suggested fix is partly unsafe as framed:
- "Match on DateDeleted timestamp": the engine never captures the bin's System.Recycle.DateDeleted. The only timestamp available is the Rust-side BATCH time (bulk.rs:394, SystemTime::now()), which is a different clock/value from the shell's per-item DateDeleted (different rounding, sub-second skew, recorded at IFileOperation completion). Requiring batch-timestamp == DateDeleted would FAIL legitimate restores — a false-negative that breaks the whole restore feature. This variant is not safe.
- "Populate recycle_bin_id with $I/$R identity": requires capturing the destination name at delete time, which the current shell layer doesn't do. Capturing it post-PerformOperations re-introduces the exact same multiplicity/race problem (which fresh $R* is mine?), made worse by the 8-worker parallel trash pool (trash.rs:101-123). Doing it correctly needs IFileOperationProgressSink::PostDeleteItem machinery that doesn't exist — non-trivial, and the naive version is racy in precisely the scenario it targets.

NET: a genuine but low-impact correctness edge case (wrong-version-restored, both copies preserved), with a remediation whose simplest forms would break legitimate restores. It does not rise to the data-integrity/data-loss "real" bar, but it is a true latent ambiguity, hence uncertain rather than fully refuted. Relevant files: C:/Users/adamm/Desktop/Code/FileID/platforms/windows/src/engine/src/commands/trash.rs, .../commands/bulk.rs, .../commands/trash_log.rs, .../shell/trash.rs.

contested.title: StartScan does not interlock against in-flight face clustering — concurrent face_prints DELETE+reinsert leaves persons.representative_face_id dangling
contested.description: handle_run_face_clustering deliberately releases the single-writer lock during PHASE 2 (cluster()+consolidate(), the lock-free window the wipe interlock was added to guard, wipe.rs:37-51). But the StartScan dispatch (main.rs:549-560) does NOT check face_cluster_active, so a new scan can run while clustering is in its lock-free phase. During that window a dbwriter batch (dbwriter.rs:345-397) that re-processes a face-bearing file with faces_evaluated=true runs `DELETE FROM face_prints WHERE file_id=?` and re-inserts the faces with fresh AUTOINCREMENT ids, committing on the writer mutex. Clustering then re-acquires the lock in PHASE 3 and executes `INSERT INTO persons (... representative_face_id = anchor.anchor_face_id ...)` (face_clustering.rs:338-355) using anchor_face_id values read back in PHASE 1 — some of which the concurrent scan batch just deleted. persons.representative_face_id has no foreign key (only idx_person_repface, migrations.rs:184/200), so the INSERT silently succeeds with a dangling rep face id, and the follow-up `UPDATE face_prints SET person_id=?1 WHERE id=?2` (face_clustering.rs:360-364) for those assignments matches 0 rows, leaving the newly re-inserted faces orphaned with person_id=NULL. Result: People cards that point at non-existent crops/faces and clusters missing their re-scanned members — the same phantom-person/dangling-rep class the wipe<->cluster interlock fixes, but reached via the un-interlocked scan path instead of wipe.
contested.trigger: On a large over-split library, face clustering auto-fires after a scan and runs for several seconds. The local user starts another scan (or re-scan of a subfolder) while that clustering is still in its lock-free cluster()+consolidate() phase; a re-processed face-bearing file's faces_evaluated batch deletes+reinserts face_prints, after which clustering's PHASE-3 persist writes persons rows whose representative_face_id references the deleted face ids.
contested.suggested_fix: Apply the same interlock the wipe handler uses. Either have the StartScan dispatch bounce/wait when face_cluster_active is set (mirror of main.rs:787-798), or have face clustering PHASE 3 re-validate anchor_face_id / assignment face ids against the current face_prints (e.g. only INSERT persons whose anchor_face_id still exists, and skip assignments whose face id was deleted). Re-reading the anchor face ids under the PHASE-3 lock before the persist closes the window.

verdicts.reasoning:
I independently re-derived the full chain and every link holds.

ASYMMETRY IS REAL. wipe is interlocked against in-flight clustering (wipe.rs:37-51 waits on face_cluster_active for exactly this lock-free-window reason), and RunFaceClustering is single-flighted (main.rs:787-798). But the StartScan dispatch (main.rs:549-560) does NOT consult face_cluster_active — verified by grepping every use of that flag (only wipe.rs + the cluster single-flight/wipe dispatch reference it). So a scan and a clustering pass can legitimately overlap; only scan-vs-scan is gated (scan.rs:38-57 scan_already_running).

LOCK-FREE WINDOW IS REAL. handle_run_face_clustering deliberately releases the writer mutex for PHASE 2 cluster()+consolidate() (face_clustering.rs:22-28, 171, 174-282) and only re-acquires it for the PHASE 3 persist (288).

STALE ANCHORS ARE USED VERBATIM. anchor.anchor_face_id originates from PHASE-1 reads (face_clustering.rs pipeline line 195 faces[best_idx].face_id, carried through consolidate line 449) and is written into persons.representative_face_id at commands/face_clustering.rs:351 with NO re-validation against current face_prints. The follow-up UPDATE face_prints SET person_id=?1 WHERE id=?2 (360-364) keys on the same stale assignment face ids.

THE CHURN IS REAL. dbwriter flush (dbwriter.rs:175 same Arc<Mutex<Connection>>, 345-397) runs DELETE FROM face_prints WHERE file_id=? then re-inserts with fresh AUTOINCREMENT ids for any faces_evaluated file, committing under the same mutex. face_prints.id is INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT (migrations.rs:192) so deleted ids never recur. A rescan-a-subfolder (rescan=true, SettingsView.xaml.cs:557 → scan_session.rs:161-163 empties the skip set) re-processes face-bearing anchor files, so the deleted ids genuinely overlap the in-flight anchors.

NO GUARD ON THE COLUMN. persons.representative_face_id has no FK, only idx_person_repface (migrations.rs:186/200), so the dangling INSERT silently succeeds even with PRAGMA foreign_keys=ON (db/mod.rs:29). Result: a persons row whose rep face id is gone, plus re-inserted faces left person_id=NULL because the PHASE-3 UPDATE matched 0 rows.

HARM CONFIRMED AND PERSISTENT. A dangling rep drops the person from the merge-suggestions INNER JOIN (bulk.rs:701-706 ON rep.id=p.representative_face_id), and the orphaned faces are missing from their cluster. Critically the auto-heal is gated: the corrupting scan's ScanComplete fires AutoTriggerFaceClusteringAsync (EngineClient.cs:1104) but _faceClusterAutoInFlight is still held by the first pass (1273-1277), so it skips; after the first pass releases the gate (1137) nothing re-fires. The bad state survives until a manual re-cluster or another scan.

THREAT-MODEL FIT: this is a local-user timing race, not attacker/content-controlled — but the lens here is reachability of a data-integrity defect, and it is reachable by normal UI actions (rescan a subfolder while post-scan clustering runs). It is the same transaction-atomicity class the maintainers already chose to interlock for wipe, reached via the un-interlocked scan path.

Confidence is held at ~0.68 (not higher) ONLY because of timing tightness: the second scan must reach a face-bearing batch commit within clustering's multi-second PHASE 2 window, including a fresh per-scan ModelStack::load_default (scan.rs:161-163). That window is narrow, most plausible on the rescan-subfolder/large-over-split-library combination the claim names. The defect is genuine and the suggested fix (gate StartScan on face_cluster_active, or re-read anchor ids under the PHASE-3 lock) is correct and does not break legitimate cases. Severity medium and category transaction-atomicity are accurate.

The mechanism is real but the harm is not. Verified facts: clustering drops the writer lock during PHASE 2 (face_clustering.rs:171 drop(conn), re-acquired at :288); DbWriter shares the same Arc<Mutex<Connection>> single-writer (dbwriter.rs:58) and its faces_evaluated path does DELETE+reinsert with fresh ids (dbwriter.rs:345-397); StartScan dispatch (main.rs:549-560) does NOT check face_cluster_active (only wipe.rs:47-51 does); persons.representative_face_id has no FK (migrations.rs:186, only idx at :200). So a concurrent scan batch CAN delete+reinsert face_prints during the lock-free window and PHASE 3 CAN store a dangling representative_face_id.

But tracing to actual stored/displayed state shows the consequence is benign and self-correcting, not corruption/data-loss/panic/telemetry:

1) No phantom cards. The People-tab listing query (PeopleViewModel.cs:180-183) is `FROM persons p JOIN face_prints fp ON fp.person_id = p.id ... GROUP BY p.id` — an INNER JOIN. A person whose members were all deleted yields zero joined rows and is excluded; it never surfaces as a card. The findMergeSuggestions query (bulk.rs:702-705) also INNER-JOINs on representative_face_id, filtering dangling-rep persons.

2) Dangling rep is cosmetic. The COALESCE at PeopleViewModel.cs:174-179 may return the dangling id, used only to load face_crops/<id>.jpg — at worst a blank thumbnail, not lost/wrong data. Member count and name come from live joins, not the stale stored value.

3) Re-inserted faces are the explicitly documented-benign case: PHASE 3 first runs UPDATE face_prints SET person_id=NULL (:292), so the scan's new faces (new ids not in `assignments`) land with person_id=NULL — the comment at face_clustering.rs:22-28 ("picked up next run").

4) No panic/no rollback/no corruption: the UPDATE...WHERE id=? matching 0 rows is not an error; there is no FK to violate; PHASE 3 commits cleanly (:367). No network/telemetry.

5) Self-healing: the same scan B that caused the race fires AutoTriggerFaceClusteringAsync() on its own ScanComplete (EngineClient.cs:1081-1104), which re-reads current face_prints and rebuilds all persons, overwriting any transient dangling rep / NULL assignment (the engine single-flight at main.rs:787-798 just defers it until A's pass finishes).

This is materially weaker than the wipe<->cluster case the interlock guards: a wipe reports "empty library" success and has no corrective re-cluster, so ghost persons persist as visible garbage; the scan path always ends with a corrective re-cluster and the listing query structurally hides the transient artifacts. The suggested fix (bounce/wait scans on face_cluster_active) would also degrade a legitimate workflow — blocking the user's scan during the multi-second post-scan auto-cluster on every large library. Net: a transient, invisible, self-correcting artifact, not persisted wrong state, data loss, corruption, panic, or telemetry. Confidence held below 0.9 because the narrow timing window is genuinely reachable and one could argue a momentary blank anchor thumbnail is a minor UX blemish — but it does not meet the data-loss/integrity bar of this lens.

The race MECHANISM is real and I confirmed every link: StartScan dispatch (main.rs:549-560) does NOT check face_cluster_active (only WipeLibrary 807-818 and RunFaceClustering 787-798 do); clustering deliberately drops the writer lock during PHASE 2 (face_clustering.rs:171 drop(conn) → 288 re-db.lock()); PHASE 3 INSERTs persons.representative_face_id = anchor.anchor_face_id (line 351) and UPDATE face_prints ... WHERE id = a.face_id (line 363) using ids captured in PHASE 1; dbwriter's faces_evaluated path DELETEs face_prints WHERE file_id=? then re-INSERTs with fresh AUTOINCREMENT ids (dbwriter.rs:345-397); both run on the same Arc<Mutex<Connection>> (scan.rs:23/276-283 vs face_clustering re-lock); and representative_face_id has no FK, only idx_person_repface (migrations.rs:186/200), so a dangling-rep INSERT succeeds silently. So a local user starting a scan during the few-second post-scan auto-clustering window can produce a dangling rep + re-inserted faces stranded at person_id=NULL.

But under the skeptical/fix-safety lens it is NOT a genuine medium bug: (1) The outcome is not data-loss/corruption/telemetry/engine-panic. A dangling representative_face_id is a soft link with no FK; read queries either inner-join it away (suggested-merges INNER JOIN face_prints rep ON rep.id=p.representative_face_id, bulk.rs:701-706, simply excludes that person) or render a missing crop. The DB stays structurally valid; nothing panics. (2) It self-heals within seconds: the same scan that caused the churn auto-fires its own clustering pass on completion (EngineClient.cs:1104), which reloads ALL faces unconditionally (face_clustering.rs:52-55, no person_id filter), does DELETE FROM persons (293) + full rebuild, re-assigns the NULL faces, and writes a fresh valid rep — exactly the invariant the PHASE-1 comment already states (lines 26-28). The auto-gate is released on FaceClusteringComplete/failed (EngineClient.cs:1128/1137), so the follow-up pass isn't suppressed. (3) The headline suggested fix — bounce/wait StartScan when face_cluster_active — would FALSE-POSITIVE on a common legitimate workflow: clustering auto-fires after EVERY scan, so a user re-scanning a subfolder shortly after a prior scan would routinely hit a spurious scan_already_busy rejection. Wipe can afford to wait because it is rare and destructive; gating the hot, repeated scan path the same way regresses a normal flow. The narrower variant (re-read anchor/assignment ids under the PHASE-3 lock) is harmless polish, but the issue as framed (medium severity, dangling-rep/orphan harm) overstates a transient, self-correcting, non-corrupting condition.

contested.title: restoreFromTrash claims success when the original path is already occupied — wrong-bytes persistence / silent data loss
contested.description: The Recycle Bin restore does NO pre-restore existence check at `original_path`, and its only success criterion is `if !Path::new(original_path).exists() { bail }` AFTER the shell verb runs. When the user has re-created a file at the same path since trashing (e.g. trashed `report.docx`, later saved a new `report.docx` there, then pressed Ctrl+Z / Restore), `InvokeVerb('Undelete')` to an occupied destination is non-deterministic under `-NonInteractive`: on common builds the shell suppresses the conflict UI and the undelete is a NO-OP (the recycle-bin item is never restored), yet `original_path` still exists (the unrelated newer file), so the function returns Ok(). handle_restore_from_trash then counts it as `succeeded` and re-INSERTs a `files` row for `original_path`. Net result: the user's actually-trashed bytes remain stranded in the Recycle Bin, the library now references a DIFFERENT file under the restored item's identity, and the UI reports a successful restore. On builds where the shell instead overwrites the occupied destination, the newer user file is destroyed. Either branch is silent wrong-persistence or data loss, and the path is fully under a local user's normal workflow (no attacker needed). The DB re-insert is keyed `INSERT OR IGNORE` on a UNIQUE(path_text) column, so it cannot fix the mismatch — it just silently keeps whichever row exists.
contested.trigger: Trash a file via the Cleanup/Library trash button; create or save a new file with the same name at the same folder; invoke Restore (restoreFromTrash) for that batch. The handler reports succeeded=1 while the trashed bytes are never actually restored (or the new file is clobbered).
contested.suggested_fix: Before invoking the Undelete verb, fail fast if `original_path` already exists on disk (verbatim-prefixed symlink_metadata probe), surfacing a clear 'destination occupied' message so the user resolves the collision — OR restore to a uniquified sibling name (mirroring restructure_apply::unique_destination) and re-insert the row at that actual landing path. Additionally, capture the recycle-bin item's pre-restore identity and verify post-restore that the SAME item was consumed (e.g. confirm it is gone from the bin) rather than treating mere existence of the path as proof of restore.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified the code and full call chain independently.

CHAIN: handle_trash_files (bulk.rs:305) trashes a file, DELETEs its files row, and appends a trash_log entry storing original_path (bulk.rs:367-378). handle_restore_from_trash (trash.rs:11) reads the batch and for each item calls restore_one_from_recycle_bin (trash.rs:74); on Ok it re-INSERTs a files row (trash.rs:87-99, INSERT OR IGNORE) and counts succeeded.

THE FLAW IS PRESENT. restore_one_from_recycle_bin (trash.rs:132) shells PowerShell that matches the bin item by DeletedFrom+Name and calls $i.InvokeVerb('Undelete') (trash.rs:153-158). Its ONLY post-verb success criterion is status.success() (trash.rs:176) plus `if !Path::new(original_path).exists()` (trash.rs:179-181). There is NO pre-restore occupancy check and the post check tests mere path existence, not whether the SAME bin item was consumed. So if the user re-created an unrelated file at original_path after trashing, that newer file makes .exists() true and the function returns Ok() regardless of whether the trashed bytes were restored.

REACHABILITY: Fully reachable by a normal local user under the stated threat model — trash report.docx, later save a new report.docx at the same path, press Restore/Undo. No attacker, no forged IPC. SEC-7 containment (trash.rs:46-73) only checks the destination is inside an authorized root, not occupancy. The app side (RestoreFromTrashAsync, EngineClient.Commands.cs:720) trusts the engine's succeeded/failed counts verbatim — no upstream guard.

HARM: In the realistic -NonInteractive branch, InvokeVerb('Undelete') cannot raise the shell's name-collision prompt, so the COM-automation undelete aborts as a no-op; the trashed bytes stay stranded in the bin, yet original_path exists (the new file) → Ok() → succeeded++ → files row re-INSERTed pointing at the path. The user is told "restored" while their actual trashed file is still in the bin and the library row now references DIFFERENT bytes under the restored item's identity. INSERT OR IGNORE (trash.rs:88) can only keep whichever row exists, never reconcile the mismatch. That is a genuine silent data-integrity violation surfaced as success.

CALIBRATION: I confirm the misreported-success / wrong-bytes-reference outcome (no-op branch) as a real high-impact integrity bug. I discount the claim's worst-case escalation (the newer user file being silently OVERWRITTEN/destroyed): non-interactive shell undelete to an occupied destination typically refuses rather than clobbering without a prompt, so the dominant realistic outcome is recoverable (bytes remain in the bin) rather than permanent destruction. The bug is real and worth fixing; suggested fixes (fail fast on occupied destination via verbatim symlink_metadata probe, or uniquify the landing name and re-insert at the actual path, plus verify the specific bin item was consumed) are sound. Confidence below 0.9 because the exact severity hinges on platform-specific shell behavior I can reason about but cannot execute here, and the overwrite/destruction branch is unlikely.

Traced the full call chain (handle_restore_from_trash → restore_one_from_recycle_bin in platforms/windows/src/engine/src/commands/trash.rs) to actual disk/DB consequences. The claimed "wrong-bytes persistence / silent data loss" does not hold under the data-integrity lens.

1) The engine performs NO destructive disk operation in restore. restore_one_from_recycle_bin (trash.rs:132-183) only spawns PowerShell to invoke the shell 'Undelete' verb, then probes `.exists()` (line 179). It never moves, copies, deletes, or overwrites any bytes itself. The only DB write in the entire handler is INSERT OR IGNORE (trash.rs:87-99); lines 212/220 are the unrelated handle_revert_merge. There is no DELETE/UPDATE/REPLACE against `files` in the restore path, so an existing row for the new file is left fully intact (id, tags, faces, OCR untouched).

2) The "library references a DIFFERENT file under the restored item's identity / wrong-state persisted" assertion is unfounded. When the original was trashed, bulk.rs:367 DELETEd its row and CASCADE-deleted all dependent tags/faces/OCR. The re-inserted row (trash.rs:88) is a pure path placeholder: size_bytes=0, no phash/tags/faces, kind/extension derived from the path string. With INSERT OR IGNORE on UNIQUE(path_text), it is a no-op if a row already exists (correctly keeps the new file's real row) or inserts a zeroed placeholder otherwise. Either way the row only claims "a file exists here," which is accurate regardless of bytes; the next scan repopulates real metadata. No stale content identity is carried or mis-attached.

3) The asserted shell behaviors are not the established reality. Documented Shell.Application Undelete-to-occupied-destination behavior is rename-on-collision (restored item lands at a uniquified sibling such as "name (2).ext") — winshell docs and Windows "keep both" semantics — not a silent no-op and not a silent overwrite. In that dominant case the trashed bytes ARE restored to disk and the new file survives: zero data loss. The "silently overwrites the new file" branch requires interactive consent a scripted -NonInteractive COM InvokeVerb cannot supply; suppressing the conflict UI leads to skip, not silent clobber.

4) Worst realistic outcome is cosmetic/UX: if the shell no-ops, the trashed bytes remain safely recoverable in the Recycle Bin (not lost) and the user momentarily holds their intact newer file while the UI says "restored." No corruption, no wrong-content persistence, no telemetry, no engine panic.

The project's own move logic (restructure_apply.rs:147-158 unique_destination, "real moves never clobber") shows non-clobbering is the design intent; adding the same fail-fast/uniquify to restore is a reasonable robustness/UX improvement but its absence does not cause data loss or wrong-state persistence. Confidence capped at 0.8 because exact Windows-build behavior of scripted Undelete under occupied destinations can vary; however, no variant traces to engine-driven byte loss or persisted wrong content — the worst case is the recoverable Recycle-Bin no-op.

The claim's "high / data-integrity" framing — "wrong-bytes persistence / silent data loss" with two harmful branches — does not hold up against the actual code and Windows shell semantics.

CODE VERIFIED (platforms/windows/src/engine/src/commands/trash.rs):
- restore_one_from_recycle_bin (line 132) finds the bin item by DeletedFrom-parent + name (lines 154-158), invokes $i.InvokeVerb('Undelete'), then asserts success via PowerShell exit code (line 176) AND Path::exists(original_path) (line 179). The claim accurately describes that the only post-restore signal is mere path existence.
- handle_restore_from_trash (line 74) counts succeeded and INSERT-OR-IGNOREs a files row (line 87-99). path_text is UNIQUE ON CONFLICT REPLACE (migrations.rs:137).

WHY THE HARM CLAIMS FAIL:
1. Branch B ("newer user file is destroyed / clobbered") is factually wrong about Windows. The Shell Undelete verb is a move-with-conflict-resolution; it never silently overwrites an occupied destination — it prompts or aborts/renames. The shell never clobbers. So the data-destruction branch is not a real outcome.
2. Branch A ("undelete no-ops, succeeds anyway") is at worst a cosmetic over-report. In that branch: the newer on-disk file is intact (no data loss), the trashed bytes stay in the Recycle Bin (fully recoverable via Explorer), and the DB stays consistent with disk — path_text maps to the file that is actually on disk. There is no stored content-identity (hash/inode) being violated, so "library references a DIFFERENT file under the restored item's identity" is incorrect; size_bytes=0 self-corrects on next scan. Net effect is only that the UI says "restored" for one item that wasn't actually brought back — a UX/accuracy bug, not data-loss/corruption/telemetry/panic.
3. The no-op-vs-modal-prompt behavior the claim asserts ("on common builds the shell suppresses the conflict UI and the undelete is a NO-OP") is an unverified empirical assumption; the COM Shell.Application verb may instead pop a shell-owned modal (PowerShell's -NonInteractive does not govern shell UI), yielding a hang rather than a silent success — again not data loss.

FIX-SAFETY LENS (assigned focus): The suggested fix is partly infeasible and partly risks false-positives. "Restore to a uniquified sibling name (mirror unique_destination)" cannot be done with this mechanism — InvokeVerb('Undelete') restores ONLY to the item's recorded original location; you cannot redirect the landing path without abandoning the shell-verb approach for a full IFileOperation bin-extraction (a different implementation). The "fail fast if original_path exists" half addresses only a benign over-report and would surface a "destination occupied" error in legitimate re-created-same-name workflows, trading a harmless over-report for a hard failure.

Conclusion: not data-loss, not corruption, not wrong-bytes persistence; worst case is a low-severity success over-report, and the proposed remediation is not safely/correctly implementable as described. Refute the high/data-integrity framing. Confidence is below 0.9 only because the exact shell no-op-vs-prompt behavior is environment-dependent and not provable from source.
